name: Selective Commit Migration with Cherry-Pick
description: Use git cherry-pick to selectively apply specific commits from one branch to another without merging the entire branch history.
prerequisites: Knowledge of commit hashes to cherry-pick

steps.title: Identify commits to cherry-pick
steps.description: Review the commit history of the source branch to find the specific commit hashes you want to apply to your current branch.
steps.tip: Use 'git log --oneline --grep="fix"' to filter commits by message content.

steps.title: Cherry-pick a single commit
steps.description: Apply the specified commit to your current branch. Git creates a new commit with the same changes but a different hash.

steps.title: Cherry-pick a range of commits
steps.description: Apply all commits in the range, excluding abc1234 and including def5678. Use three dots (abc1234...def5678) to include both endpoints.
steps.warning: Range cherry-pick applies commits in order. If any commit conflicts, the entire operation pauses until you resolve it.

steps.title: Resolve cherry-pick conflicts
steps.command:
git status
# Fix conflicts in listed files
git add .
git cherry-pick --continue
steps.description: If a cherry-pick causes conflicts, resolve them, stage the changes, and continue. Use --abort to cancel the entire cherry-pick operation.

steps.title: Cherry-pick without committing
steps.description: Stage the changes from multiple commits without creating individual commits. This lets you combine them into a single commit with your own message.
steps.tip: This is useful when you want to cherry-pick several small fixes and bundle them into one clean commit.

steps.title: Track cherry-pick origin with -x
steps.description: The -x flag appends '(cherry picked from commit abc1234)' to the commit message, making it easy to trace the original source.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# cherry-pick-workflow.sh — Cherry-pick commits between branches
set -euo pipefail

SOURCE_BRANCH="${1:?Usage: $0 <source-branch> [commit-hash...]}"
shift
COMMITS=("$@")

CURRENT_BRANCH=$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD)
echo "Current branch: $CURRENT_BRANCH"
echo "Source branch:  $SOURCE_BRANCH"

if [ ${#COMMITS[@]} -eq 0 ]; then
  echo ""
  echo "=== Recent commits on $SOURCE_BRANCH ==="
  git log --oneline "$SOURCE_BRANCH" -15
  echo ""
  echo "No commit hashes provided. Re-run with specific hashes:"
  echo "  $0 $SOURCE_BRANCH <hash1> <hash2> ..."
  exit 0
fi

echo ""
echo "Commits to cherry-pick:"
for HASH in "${COMMITS[@]}"; do
  echo "  $(git log --oneline -1 "$HASH")"
done

echo ""
read -rp "Cherry-pick these commits with -x flag? (y/N) " confirm
if [[ "$confirm" =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
  for HASH in "${COMMITS[@]}"; do
    echo "Cherry-picking $HASH..."
    git cherry-pick -x "$HASH" || {
      echo "Conflict detected. Resolve and run: git cherry-pick --continue"
      exit 1
    }
  done
  echo ""
  echo "All commits cherry-picked successfully."
  git log --oneline -${#COMMITS[@]}
fi

faq.question: Does cherry-pick create a duplicate commit?
faq.answer: Yes. Cherry-pick creates a new commit with the same diff but a different SHA hash. The original commit remains on its source branch. Use the -x flag to record the origin in the commit message.

faq.question: When should I cherry-pick vs merge or rebase?
faq.answer: Cherry-pick when you need only specific commits from another branch. Use merge when you want all changes from a branch. Use rebase when you want to move your entire branch to a new base. Cherry-pick is ideal for hotfixes and selective backports.

faq.question: Can I cherry-pick a merge commit?
faq.answer: Yes, but you must specify the parent with -m. For example, 'git cherry-pick -m 1 <merge-hash>' applies the changes as seen from the first parent. This is usually what you want for standard merge commits.
